title: Swap Two Integers In Place

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-07-algorithms-numerical-methods.md

setup.narrative: Swap two integers i and j without using a third storage variable.
rules: Arithmetic: i = i + j; j = i - j (= old i); i = i - j (= old j). Risk: integer overflow.
XOR: i = i ^ j; j = j ^ i (= old i); i = i ^ j (= old j). Uses x ^ x = 0 and x ^ 0 = x.
Both achieve the swap in O(1) without extra storage.
setup.question: Show how to swap with XOR and identify the failure modes.

learningObjectives: Apply XOR identities x ^ x = 0 and x ^ 0 = x.
Recognize integer-overflow risk in the arithmetic version.
Note that XOR swap fails when i and j refer to the same memory location.

stages.label: XOR swap trace
stages.prompt: Start with i = a, j = b. After i = i ^ j: i = a ^ b, j = b. After j = j ^ i: j = b ^ (a ^ b) = ?

options.text: j = a (since b ^ b = 0 and 0 ^ a = a).

stages.explanation: j = b ^ a ^ b = a ^ (b ^ b) = a ^ 0 = a. So j now holds the original value of i. Final step: i = i ^ j = (a ^ b) ^ a = b. Both swapped.

stages.label: Commit -- failure mode
stages.prompt: When does the XOR swap FAIL?

options.text: When i and j are aliases (same memory location): i ^ j = 0 zeroes out the location, then everything stays 0. Always check for self-aliasing first.
When i and j are negative.
When i = j numerically (different locations are fine).

stages.explanation: If i and j are different variables but happen to hold the same value, the XOR swap works fine (a ^ a = 0 then 0 ^ a = a). The danger is only when they alias the SAME memory: i ^ j = 0 destroys the value entirely. The arithmetic version has the same failure mode.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Why might compilers prefer std::swap (often using a temp register) over the XOR trick? (Modern CPUs have plenty of registers; std::swap is clearer and avoids the aliasing edge case.)
Apply XOR-swap to two pointers -- does it work? (Yes, on platforms where pointer XOR is meaningful, but C standard says pointer arithmetic is undefined for non-array pointers.)
What's the equivalent for floating-point values? (No XOR-swap works; floats have a sign bit and IEEE encoding subtleties. Just use a temporary.)
